steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what point Prospero most directly makes about Miranda and her memories. Focus on Prospero's lines: he confirms her memory is real (‘Thou hadst, and more’), then reasons: ‘If thou remember'st ought ere thou camest here, / How thou camest here thou mayst.’ This is a direct if-then inference — remembering something before arrival implies she may remember the arrival itself.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Miranda's impression of a scene is vague because she is remembering a scenario she had daydreamed about as a child rather than a scenario that had occurred in reality.: Prospero explicitly confirms the memory is real (‘Thou hadst’). He does not suggest it is a daydream. ✗
  • Choice B — Miranda's reminiscences about her early childhood have a melancholy quality that betrays her discontented view of her current circumstances.: No emotional tone of melancholy or discontent appears in Prospero's lines. ✗
  • Choice C — Miranda's ability to summon details of an experience she had before arriving on the island suggests that she may also be able to summon details of her arrival on the island.: This matches Prospero's if-then logic exactly. ✓
  • Choice D — Miranda's doubts about the accuracy of one recollection of a place other than the island are clouding her judgment and seem to be making her reluctant to explore her recollection of traveling to the island.: Prospero draws an inference about what Miranda may remember; he says nothing about reluctance or doubt blocking her. ✗
That matches Choice C — Miranda's ability to summon details of an experience she had before arriving on the island suggests that she may also be able to summon details of her arrival on the island..
